author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: Thanks for the video. I think kites have potential. In their current state they are probably too dangerous and unpredictable due to changes in the wind over time. Another issue is that due to the way a kite operates, the altitude that it gains would also increase the radius of potential for damage... for example, a kite that can fly 1000 feet into the air also means that it could crash down anywhere within 1000' of its tethering point. That brings up another point, that in order for these kites to generate substantial electricity while they rotate, they would need to have fairly large wingspans... and typically the structure of something like that cannot withstand multiple impacts into the ground. While it might seem like a clever idea, I worry that it would not work well from a practical point of view. There are some things that could potentially improve the idea... But,  we need a good way to deal with the issue of wind failure. A commenter mentioned reeling it in, before the wind failed... that might work if you had sensors set up. Once reeled in, you would need a launching system once the wind picked up.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes - i tend to agree with that mate - but it is very interesting so much effort is going into them

replies.author: @Barskor1

author: @wrekced
text:
You missed a form of generation that kites could use: Triboelectric. The material of a kite vibrates in the wind. That vibration is usually lost energy; but it could be harvested with the right materials. Perhaps some peizoelectric plastic like Kynar as a tail could produce a lot of energy. You would only need to keep the kite flying. You would not need to have the kite move up and down or spin. The oscillations of the material of the body or tail would be enhanced by designing ripples into the edges of the wings or tail. A Dragonfly's wings are similar to a kite. Their wings, if you look at them carefully, are not flat on the trailing edge. It is rippled slightly like there is a little extra material along that edge. That causes the edges to oscillate in a way that improves the efficiency of the wing. Similar, I suspect to the tubercles on a whale's fin. That oscillation could drive a triboelectric harvesting device. The triboelectric generator could be added to a kite that generates other ways as well. That way you could get the most energy out of the wind that it is possible to extract.
Another idea is to just put a turbine of some sort into the body of a large box kite. Or suspend a turbine beneath a regular kite. Then you are using a kite to lift the turbine instead of a tower. Then you could raise or lower the kite to where the wind is better.

author: @daveellis9575
text: Yes, I could make this work for my home. The technology is within range of my skills and abilities, the local wind conditions in Northern Scotland are powerful and persistent, my garden is big enough and I need the power for heat on cold and windy winter days. But… planning restrictions, safety with nearby power lines, restrictions with regard to consideration of neighbours and concerns over whether power output is enough to be useful make me think I won’t do it. If it doesn’t fit my circumstances then it won’t fit anywhere for a home generation setup. It can only ever be a commercial technology, but it is a way to exploit the power of the wind at higher altitude - if adopted by a large scale setup.

author: @user-4in4nxDonaldRennie
text:
It seem you said there were only 2 ways to generate kite energy, then showed a few examples of a 3rd.
(1. Turning a drum as the kite reels out, with the drum attached to a generator. 2. Spinning the kite & cords, with the cords attached to a generator on the ground. 3. Lofting the spinning blades & generator up with the kite, and transmitting the electrical energy down along the cord.)
But maybe you meant the two methods to be;
 transmitting kinetic energy along the cord to a generator on the ground
 & lofting the generator up on the kite & transmitting electrical energy along the cord to the ground?

author: @gregweinfurtner7774
text:
I enjoy you channel very much .  But... Wind is fun to play with on a limited scale, but it not capable of a valid commercial power system that is sustainable. I should know I spent 3 years designing the perfect 3 KW wind generator, computer controlled and monitored in all aspects of its electrical and mechanical operation. An Electronic Design Specialist for 25 years, I knew what I was doing. and it was nothing but trouble. 
Wind will never be a major player in electrical generation. It's too unpredictable and can not beat the convenience of commercial generation that we've used for years. Kites are too flimsy and susceptible to  wind gusts or lack of wind, maintenance issues, etc. 
By the way Robert, do you realize that net zero CO2 means that all vegetation will die off if there is no Co2? No vegetation, no CO2 conversion to Oxygen, No humans or animals.
The best thing to do is plant more trees and crops! 
Even if you reached net zero CO2, it is such a small part of greenhouse gases, it would make very little difference in the faux global climate.

Genesis 8:22 While the earth remaineth, seedtime and harvest, and cold and heat, and summer and winter, and day and night shall not cease.

replies.text: ​@j.christie2594 I can imagine an old Miller staring at a Bridge rectifier and a bank of capacitors instead of bags of flour, as he pointed the sails of his windmill into the wind, whilst Constable sat outside working on a painting of the area. No longer is he working in a cloud of flour dust. Revolving Windmills are perhaps the best idea for squirrelling winds that plague the lower levels, due to trees, mountains, valleys, Planes, housing, buildings, tall people, cars and maybe even farts, it's messy on a gigantic scale.

replies.text: If the wind drops you just reel them in, this keeps them airbourne all the way back to base. It's similar to the winch-launch of gliders. As long as you can control the angle of attack its easy. One of the bigger problems, with long tethers, is the length/mass of the tether itself.

author: @TimeSurfer206
text:
One of the issues with wind power is the power available at ground is a mere fraction of what is often available even 50 or 100 feet up (About 10 to 20 bathtubs, for non-Yanks). That is why the kite is intriguing, but, often there isn't enough wind to lift the kite there to tap it.
So, we make one of those circular kites... And instead of putting a hole in the middle we put a weather balloon...
My brain hurts now. I'm going to poke some smot.
I remember an article in Popular Science or Mechanics, don't remember which, about a drone, basically, about the length and width of a locomotive engine (I think), with YOOGE! fans on the side.
It had a tether to the ground, and the motors were also generators.
The idea was to lift it with the fans as motors up to the Jet Stream, switch the fans to Turbine mode, and send power to the ground. 
This is a little beyond my capacities, and, even if it was, I live in the flight path of SeaTac.

You need a Ground an antenna, but 25/7 - 365.
With 3D printer you can make these, Lasersaber has STL File for smaller and big one. If you can poke smot and draw 3D, you can design one.

author: @totherarf
text:
I can envision another type of kite.
A wind sail in the shape of a torus would allow a lift force to get into the air. Having a HAWT fitted within the torus to actually generate power. This could then be fed back town to earth at high voltage/ low current giving low csa of wires, which would need to be steel cable types.
This would have the advantage of getting up high where the wind speed is both higher and more predictable. It would not be killing birds and the noise problem just isn't!
As an added "Brucie Bonus" the wing could act as a funnel to increase the effective swept area of the turbine!

Once again, thanks for tickling the Grey Stuff Rob!

replies.author: @spex357
replies.text: Lets say we all want a kite system on the street we live on,  That's 434 kites squirrelling around the sky like demented aliens, all day. The room needed would be massive, and would stop the drone delivery service they want. The noise they make is huge, and then we have a singing line playing a C# note all day. There is no kite that is happy in all winds, so you might need half a dozen different ones. If your in a high wind area, constant 60mph+ the Delta Fishing kite from New Zealand  is worth a try. 60mph for most kites leads to a snapped line or broken spars, and a stretched arm. The Rokkaku, Delta and Dopero are all easy makeable, the cell kites not so.@Warp9pnt9
